title: Matthew 2
subtitle: The Magi; Flight to Egypt; The Slaughter of the Innocents; Return to Nazareth

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — The Magi: Star, Worship, and Herod's Plot

heb.paragraph: Not 'kings' (a later tradition) but μάγοι — Persian-Babylonian scholar-priests skilled in astrology, dream interpretation, and sacred texts. Matthew's use of the term signals that Gentile seekers from the East recognise what Jerusalem's own scholars miss. The irony is deliberate.

Used three times in this passage (vv.2, 8, 11). The word ranges from social respect to divine worship. When Herod claims he wants to 'worship' the child (v.8), he uses the same verb the Magi use genuinely (v.11). The identical word masks opposite intentions — homage versus homicide.

hist.context: Matthew 2 is built around four OT fulfilment quotations — more than any other chapter. The Magi scene (vv.1–12) establishes the central irony of the chapter: Gentile outsiders from the east recognise and worship the King of the Jews, while the official Jewish establishment in Jerusalem is "disturbed" (v.3). Herod knows where the Messiah is to be born (the scholars can tell him precisely) but uses that knowledge to plot murder rather than worship.

refs.note: Bethlehem as the birthplace of the ruler who will shepherd Israel — quoted v.6. Matthew adds a phrase from 2 Sam 5:2 ("who will shepherd my people Israel"), combining both texts.
A star shall come out of Jacob — Balaam's oracle. The Magi's star may be the fulfilment of this prophecy, connecting Israel's Gentile opponents in Numbers to the Gentile worshippers in Matthew.
Gold and frankincense from the nations — Isaiah's vision of Gentiles bringing tribute to Zion. Matthew's Magi enact this vision.

poi.name: Jerusalem
poi.text: The Magi come to Jerusalem first — the obvious place to find a king. Herod's court is in Jerusalem; the chief priests and scribes are there. The city that should welcome the Messiah is instead the site of the plot against him.

poi.name: Bethlehem
poi.coords: Judean hill country, c.8km south of Jerusalem
poi.text: Bethlehem is identified from Micah 5:2 by the Jerusalem scribes. The Magi travel the short distance south. The Messiah is born not in the capital but in the village — a geographical humility that the whole narrative of Matthew will sustain.

poi.name: Star's direction
poi.coords: From east; possibly Persia, Babylon, or Arabia
poi.text: The Magi come from "the east" — the direction of the Babylonian diaspora, Persia, and Arabia. Their journey reverses the direction of the Exile: the east sends wise men westward to worship the one born King of the Jews.

notes.note: Calvin: God raised up heathen men from the east to seek his Son, thus shaming the indifference of those who had the prophets and the law. The Gentiles hasten to worship; Israel's leaders sit in Jerusalem and do nothing. The scandal of Israel's indifference is the chapter's governing irony.

Calvin on the slaughter of the innocents: God permitted this dreadful crime for his own purposes. The souls of the murdered infants entered immediately into the heavenly kingdom. Herod's cruelty served despite itself to glorify the infant king by making him the cause of the first martyrs.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on magoi discusses the term's range: royal advisors, dream interpreters, astrologers. The key point is that they represent the Gentile world pursuing the one the Jewish establishment is ignoring — the irony that governs Matthew's opening chapters.

The NET note on 'out of Egypt I called my son' traces Hosea 11:1. Matthew uses corporate typology: Israel's son-status prefigures the Messiah's son-status. Jesus relives Israel's defining moments as the true Israel.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The Magi (magoi) were a priestly caste from Persia or Babylon skilled in astronomy and dream interpretation. Their following of the star is not mere superstition but genuine inquiry. Their arrival with royal gifts signals the Gentile world acknowledging Israel's Messiah.

The star that led the Magi to the specific house is distinct from the star they saw at rising — a remarkable final directional sign. The falling down and worshipping (proskunein) is the vocabulary of divine homage, not respectful greeting.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: God permitted the Magi to be led first by a star, accommodating himself to their custom. But he would not suffer them to depart without instruction from the prophets. The star leads to Jerusalem; the Scripture leads to Bethlehem — natural and special revelation working together.

Leo the Great: The Magi fell down and worshipped him who lay in the manger, not seeing a king in a palace but adoring him wrapped in swaddling clothes. They recognised the power beneath the weakness, the majesty beneath the humility.

sections.header: Verses 13–18 — Flight to Egypt; The Massacre of the Innocents

heb.paragraph: Matthew's signature word — used 16 times for OT fulfilment. Here it introduces Hosea 11:1 ('out of Egypt I called my son') and Jeremiah 31:15 (Rachel weeping). Plēroō means more than 'prediction came true'; it means the OT pattern reaches its fullest meaning in Jesus.

Used four times in chapter 2 (vv.12, 13, 14, 22) — the chapter's structural keyword. Every major action is a withdrawal: the Magi depart another way, Joseph withdraws to Egypt, then to Israel, then to Galilee. The holy family's survival depends on strategic retreat.

hist.context: The flight to Egypt (vv.13–15) and the massacre of the innocents (vv.16–18) are Matthew's two darkest scenes — and both serve typological purposes. The flight recapitulates the Exodus (Jesus = Israel / Moses). The massacre echoes Pharaoh's killing of Hebrew infants (Exod 1:16) and the Exile's grief (Jer 31:15). Matthew is reading the birth of Jesus as the convergence point of Israel's entire history.

refs.note: Out of Egypt I called my son — originally Israel at the Exodus. Jesus recapitulates Israel's story as the true, faithful Israel.
Rachel weeping for her children — Jeremiah's lament over the Exile. Matthew applies it to the Bethlehem massacre, reading the massacre as a new Exile-level catastrophe.
Pharaoh's massacre of Hebrew infant boys. Herod mirrors Pharaoh; Jesus mirrors Moses — the one who escapes to bring liberation.

poi.name: Egypt
poi.coords: Northeastern Africa; place of Exile and refuge
poi.text: The flight to Egypt reverses Israel's Exodus and recapitulates it: Jesus goes down to Egypt as Israel went down to Egypt, then comes up again (Hos 11:1). Egypt is the place of danger (Herod), refuge (God's protection), and divine calling ("out of Egypt I called my son").

poi.name: Bethlehem — massacre
poi.text: The massacre is localized to Bethlehem and its vicinity — the same geography as the birth. The place of birth becomes the place of slaughter. Rachel weeping (Jer 31:15) was associated with Ramah near Jerusalem — Matthew applies it to Bethlehem by typological extension.

notes.note: Calvin: God directs the flight with the same precision as the fulfilment. The same divine hand that moved Israel out of Egypt now moves the Son into Egypt. The Hosea citation is not allegory but typological completion: what Egypt signified in Israel's experience, it now fulfils in the Messiah's.

Calvin: The weeping of Rachel is not mere narrative pathos but theological testimony. Matthew's citation of Jeremiah shows that the consolation promised after exile (Jer 31) passes through grief. The innocents' death is the first instalment of the cost the Messiah's coming would impose.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET note on the formula quotation: Out of Egypt I called my son (Hos 11:1) refers to Israel's exodus. Matthew applies it to Jesus by a pattern of typological fulfilment: Israel's corporate experience prefigures the Messiah's individual biography. Jesus is the true Israel, recapitulating what the nation experienced.

NET note: the Greek word anachōreō (withdraw) is used three times in ch.2 (vv.12, 13, 14) and again in 4:12. The pattern of withdrawal and fulfilment frames the infancy narrative — every threat against Jesus redirects to a new fulfilment, every danger becomes the occasion for a new prophetic correspondence.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The return from Egypt after Herod's death fulfils the typological pattern: as Israel left Egypt after Pharaoh's death, Jesus leaves after Herod's death. Robertson notes the settlement in Nazareth is guided by both the fear of Archelaus and the divine direction — providence using political danger to direct the covenant family to their appointed location.

Robertson traces 'he shall be called a Nazarene' to a cluster of prophetic texts describing the Messiah as despised and lowly rather than to a single proof-text. Nazareth's obscurity expresses in its very geography the servant character that Isaiah describes.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: Herod sought the child to kill him; God brought him back from Egypt to be killed at the appointed time, not the tyrant's time. Providence governs even the timing of redemptive suffering.

Jerome on 'he shall be called a Nazarene': The evangelist does not say this was written in one prophet but in the prophets — indicating the prophetic pattern of the despised servant, not a single text. Nazareth's obscurity expresses the servant identity Isaiah describes.

sections.header: Verses 19–23 — Return: Out of Egypt; Settled in Nazareth

poi.name: Israel / Judea vs Galilee
poi.coords: Judea under Archelaus vs Galilee under Antipas
poi.text: Joseph is warned not to return to Judea (under the brutal Archelaus) and is directed to Galilee instead. The political geography of Herod's divided kingdom shapes the Messiah's location: Galilee's Antipas is less dangerous than Archelaus's Judea.

poi.name: Nazareth
poi.text: "He will be called a Nazarene" — the settlement in Nazareth fulfils the prophetic theme of the Messiah's lowly origins. Nazareth was an obscure Galilean village with no prophetic significance of its own; its very obscurity embodies the incarnation's pattern.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament -- Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the return from Egypt: the two citation formulas in Matthew 2 (that it might be fulfilled) mark every movement of the infant Jesus as prophetically directed. Robertson notes Matthew is demonstrating that Jesus's biography is the fulfilment of Israel's biography -- the son of God recapitulating the nation's story.

Robertson on Nazareth: Matthew gives a compressed citation ('he will be called a Nazarene') that does not correspond to any single OT text. Robertson traces this as a collective allusion to the prophetic theme of the Messiah as despised and rejected -- the Nazarene geography encodes the incarnation's humiliation.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas, Catena Aurea (Golden Chain) -- Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena records the patristic reading of the return from Egypt as the reversal of the Exodus: Israel went to Egypt in Jacob's time and came out under Moses; now the Son of God reverses the journey. Chrysostom notes the Messiah's biography is the covenant people's biography compressed into one life.

The Catena traces the Nazareth settlement as the fulfilment of the prophetic portrait of a despised Messiah. Origen: what the prophets said collectively about the branch (netser, Isa 11:1) and the servant's rejection is concentrated geographically in the name Nazarene.

calvin.source: John Calvin, Commentary on the Harmony of the Evangelists -- Public Domain

notes.note: Calvin: God appears to Joseph in a dream at every critical juncture -- birth, flight, return, settlement. The pattern establishes divine governance over every movement of the infant Jesus. No step of the early life is humanly accidental; each is divinely directed.

Calvin on Nazareth: Matthew does not cite a specific prophet but the whole body of prophetic testimony that described the Messiah as despised and rejected. Nazareth was a byword for insignificance; settling there was the incarnation's consistent choice of the marginal place.

netbible.source: NET Bible Notes -- Scripture quotations marked (NET) are from the NET Bible

notes.note: The NET note on the fulfilment citations in ch.2 discusses the four OT quotations and their interpretive logic: Matthew is reading the OT typologically -- events in Israel's history prefigure events in the Messiah's life because the Messiah is Israel's representative.

The NET note on 'He will be called a Nazarene' discusses the absence of a direct OT source. The note surveys the major options: a collective allusion to the prophetic branch (netser, Isa 11:1), a wordplay on Nazareth's insignificance, or a reference to an apocryphal source. The collective allusion to prophetic rejection is most likely.

heb.paragraph: Matthew says the prophets predicted he would be called a Nazarene, yet no OT verse says this exactly. The likely wordplay is with nēṣer (branch/shoot) from Isaiah 11:1 — the messianic 'shoot' from Jesse's stump settles in a town whose name sounds like 'branch.' Matthew finds prophecy in a pun.

Distinguished from paroikeō ('sojourn temporarily'). The family settles permanently in Nazareth — this is not a stopover but a new home. The verb quietly establishes Galilee, not Bethlehem, as Jesus' base of operations for the rest of the Gospel.

hist.context: The return from Egypt (vv.19–23) completes the Exodus typology: as Israel came up out of Egypt into the promised land, so Jesus comes up from Egypt into Israel. But the destination is Galilee, not Judea — a detail Matthew uses to fulfil "he will be called a Nazarene." The move to Galilee also sets up 4:12–17, where Jesus's Galilean ministry fulfils Isaiah's "Galilee of the Gentiles."

refs.note: Go back to Egypt, for all those who were trying to take your life are dead — God's words to Moses almost verbatim. The angel's words to Joseph in v.20 echo this precisely, underlining the Moses typology.
A shoot from the stump of Jesse / a Branch (nēṣer) from his roots. The wordplay on Nazareth/Nazarene may connect to this — the "Branch" prophecy pointing to the Davidic king from Jesse's line.

ppl.name: The Magi
ppl.text: The first worshippers in Matthew — foreshadowing "all nations" of 28:19.

ppl.name: Herod the Great
ppl.text: Knows where the Messiah should be born; uses the knowledge to attempt murder. Mirror of Pharaoh.

ppl.name: Joseph
ppl.text: Four dreams, four acts of obedience. A second Joseph — the one who first went down to Egypt.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Out of Egypt I called my son."Hos 11:1Originally about Israel's Exodus. Matthew reads it typologically: Jesus recapitulates Israel's entire history as the true, faithful Son where Israel was faithless.

src.title: Herod the Great
src.quote: Roman client-king of Judea, known for grandiose building projects and violent paranoia. He executed several of his own sons. The massacre of Bethlehem's infant boys fits his documented character exactly.
src.note: Josephus confirms Herod's murderous paranoia though doesn't record this specific event.

src.title: The Magi's star
src.quote: Various proposals: conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn (7 BC), a nova, Halley's Comet (12 BC), or a supernatural phenomenon. Matthew does not identify it astronomically — its function in the narrative is to lead and to stop.
src.note: The star's movement (going ahead of them, stopping) is not astronomical behaviour.

rec.text: The Magi's visit has been celebrated as Epiphany (6 January) since at least the 4th century — the manifestation of Christ to the Gentiles.
The massacre of the Holy Innocents (28 December in the Western calendar) is among the oldest commemorations in the Christian calendar.

rows.label: The Magi and the Scribes: Knowledge Without Response
rows.text: The Magi travel from afar; the chief priests and scribes know the location from prophecy but do not go. The contrast is the chapter's theological centre: secular knowledge travels; informed knowledge stays home.

rows.label: The Magi and the Scribes: Knowledge Without Response
rows.text: The Magi travel from afar; the chief priests and scribes know the location from prophecy but do not go. The contrast is the chapter's theological centre: secular knowledge travels; informed knowledge stays home.

rows.label: New Exodus: Jesus Reliving Israel's Story — 2:13-21
rows.text: Flight to Egypt and return recapitulate the Exodus. Jesus is the true Israel, reliving the nation's defining journey.

tx.title: 'Out of Egypt I called my son' (Hos 11:1)
tx.content: MT: mimitzerayim qarati livni. Hosea 11:1 originally refers to Israel's Exodus. Matthew applies it to Jesus.
tx.note: Matthew uses corporate typology: Israel's son-status prefigures the Messiah's son-status. Jesus recapitulates Israel's defining historical moments as the true Israel.

positions.name: Collective reference to the prophetic pattern
positions.argument: No single OT text says 'he shall be called a Nazarene.' Matthew refers to the prophetic pattern of the despised servant.

positions.name: Reference to Isa 11:1 specifically
positions.argument: The Hebrew netser (branch) in Isa 11:1 is the closest single-text connection to Nazareth.

themes.note: Matthew 2 establishes governing patterns: Gentiles respond while Israel's leaders do not; Jesus relives Israel's story as the true Israel; and Providence governs every detail of the Messiah's path including the escape routes.
